title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption: Between 1971 and 2015, the total number of global parties signed on to multilateral environmental agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues shows a general upward trend across all listed conventions. The vertical axis measures the count of parties from 0 to 200, while the horizontal axis spans the years from 1971 to 2015. The Vienna Convention (Ozone) and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) display the earliest and steepest accumulation of signatories, both reaching the 200-party mark by the end of the period. The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the UN Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD) follow a similar trajectory, rising rapidly after their inception in the early 1990s to join the top tier near 200 parties. The Kyoto Protocol (Greenhouse gas emissions) and the World Heritage Convention show steady growth, ending the period in the range of 190 to 195 parties. In the middle of the distribution, the Cites Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora and the Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants reach between 170 and 180 parties. The Ramsar Convention (Wetlands) and the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety end near 170 parties. The UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) and the Rotterdam Convention (Hazardous chemicals) show slower initial uptake, finishing between 150 and 165 parties. The Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals records the lowest total, ending just above 120 parties. The lines show that while the timing of adoption varies, the majority of these agreements converge toward high levels of global participation over time.
